coredata.dc:title: Novel surfactant-free electro-nanofabricating strategy for highly sensitive and selective colorectal cancer MicroRNA analysis

coredata.dc:description: A label-free nanostructured electrochemical biosensor utilizing graphene quantum dot (GQD) decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), known as Au-GQD-NS, is presented to colorectal cancer (CRC) biomarker early detection, micro RNA-223 (miR-223). Thiolated DNA probes (Cap-223) are immobilized onto a fabricated and highly conductive nanostructure platform, which improves accessibility and miR-223 recognition. To analyze the nanostructured surface, various sophisticated techniques such as FESEM, EDAX, AFM, and electrochemical approaches are employed. Electrochemical approaches including DPV, EIS, and CV are applied to investigate the electroactivity of substrate, immobilization of Cap-223 on Au-GQD-NPs and subsequent hybridization with the miR-223. The electro-nanofabricated biosensor showcases a wide linear detection range (zepto M to nano M) with an impressively suitable LOD (0.024 atto M). The biosensor selectivity is demonstrated through discrimination studies between target and mismatched miRNA sequences (miR-9 and miR-21). This biosensor also exhibits significant potential for quantitative evaluation of miR-223 in real samples, enabling its applications in point-of-care (POC) detection of CRC. In addition, the Au-GQD-NS substrate is nano-fabricated in absent of surfactants and stabilizers, providing secure anchors for immobilization of cap-223. The well-established strategies for cap-223 immobilization and hybridization with miR, combined with superior electron exchange rate compared to uncovered FTO, upgrade the biosensor’s execution. Significantly, the biosensor demonstrates efficient miR-223 detection in human serum. Overall, this nano-fabricated biosensor shows great promise for miR-based early detection of CRC, and therefore, offering great sensitivity, selectivity, and favorable hydrophilicity for translational medicine investigation and clinical applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showcasing the step-by-step electrochemical fabrication of the biosensor for miR-223 detection. The process includes immobilization of cap-223 on Au-GQD-NS surface, blocking the active surface with β-ME to prevent non-specific binding of cap-223, and the final step of hybridization of miR-223 with cap-223.

Sequential characterization of GQDs: a) UV–vis absorption spectra showcasing the absorption features of GQDs, b) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs at varying excitation wavelengths, c) Raman spectrum detailing the vibrational modes of GQDs, d) FTIR spectra illustrating the chemical composition of GQDs formed under different conditions, including the prominent C = O peak at ∼ 1641 cm−1. and finally, e) presents the size distribution of GQDs synthesized.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the graphene quantum dot (GQD) on the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate in green and the gold-coated GQD-FTO substrate in red. The peaks indicate the crystal structure and orientation of the materials. The presence of gold coating on the substrate results in a slight increase in peak intensity, suggesting improved crystallinity and possibly enhanced electronic properties. XRD is a powerful technique used to analyze the structural properties of materials at the atomic scale, allowing for the identification of different phases and the estimation of crystal parameters.

FESEM images and EDAX patterns revealing the nanofabricated surface containing GQDs nanosphere and Au nanoparticles on FTO substrate. a-c) FESEM images showing the nanostructures at different scales: a) 1 µm, b) 500 nm, c) 200 nm for GQD-FTO. d-f) FESEM images depicting the nanostructures at various scales: d) 1 µm, e) 500 nm, f) 200 nm for GQD-Au-FTO. i) EDAX pattern for GQD-FTO and h) EDAX pattern for GQD-Au-FTO. The FESEM images provide a visual representation of the morphological characteristics of the nanostructures at different scales, allowing for the estimation of their size and shape. The EDAX patterns, on the other hand, give information about the elemental composition of the samples, revealing the presence of GQDs and Au nanoparticles on the FTO substrate. The peaks in the EDAX spectra can be used to quantify the relative amounts of each element present, providing insights into the distribution and composition of the nanostructures on the surface.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of graphene quantum dots on fluorine-doped tin oxide (GQD-FTO) substrate (a,b) (scale: 10 *10 µm); and gold-coated graphene quantum dots on fluorine-doped tin oxide (Au-GQD-FTO) substrate (c,d) (scale: 20*20 µm). The AFM images provide a topographical analysis of the surface of GQD-Au NS, allowing for the estimation of GQD-Au nanostructure features and potential characterization of the materials. The results from AFM imaging of Au and GQD reveal information on the surface morphology, roughness, and size distribution of the nanoparticles, which can provide valuable insights into their properties and potential applications in various fields.

Electrochemical characterization of bare FTO and modified electrodes with GQD-Au NS: (a) cyclic voltammetry (CV), (b) differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and (c) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The green curve represents the bare Au-GQD-NSs-FTO electrode, the red curve represents cap-223, and the blue curve represents 0.024 aM of miR-223. The addition of Au and GQD nanoparticles to the FTO electrode resulted in enhanced electroactivity, as indicated by the peaks observed in the CV, EIS and DPV measurements. The presence of the analyte molecules (cap-223 and miR-223) led to changes in the electrochemical response, with either an increase or inhibition of the electroactivity, depending on the concentration of the analyte.

DPV and EIS measurements of the nanostructured biosensor for various concentrations (a, and b) and calibration curve of different concentrations (c). Each data point represents the average of at least three repetitions (n = 3). The nanostructured biosensor exhibits changes in its electrochemical behavior as concentrations increase, as evidenced by the shift of Z' Ω (EIS) that represents the impedance magnitude at different frequencies, while the −Z'' Ω (Y axis) in the EIS plot corresponds to the phase angle of the impedance. The DPV plot shows the peak current (I delta) on the y-axis against the logarithm of concentration on the x-axis for the analyte.

DPV and EIS measurements of the Au-GQD nanostructured biosensor for the detection of miR-223 and non-complementary miRNAs (miR-21 & 9). The red curve represents the detection of miR-223 at 10 pM concentration, while the blue and green curves represent the detection of 10 pM of miR-9 and miR-21, respectively. The pink curve corresponds to the detection of miR-223. The results show that the biosensor has a high sensitivity and specificity for miR-223 detection compared to non-complementary miRNAs, as indicated by the distinct peaks and curves observed in the DPV and EIS measurements.

Detection of miR-223 in human serum using our nanostructured biosensor. DPV, and EIS measurements are shown for the blank sample, Bare-FTO electrode (blue curve), cap-223 immobilized electrode (red curve), human serum sample without mir-223 (light green curve), and human serum sample spiked with mir-223 (pink curve). The results indicate successful detection and quantification of mir-223 in human serum using our biosensor technology. The red curve shows a significant increase in current response indicative of the presence of mir-223 in the serum sample.
Comparison of Different miR-223 Concentrations on Nanostructured Biosensor and its CV%, RSD%, and SD.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A label-free nanostructured electrochemical biosensor utilizing graphene quantum dot (GQD) decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), known as Au-GQD-NS, is presented to colorectal cancer (CRC) biomarker early detection, micro RNA-223 (miR-223). Thiolated DNA probes (Cap-223) are immobilized onto a fabricated and highly conductive nanostructure platform, which improves accessibility and miR-223 recognition. To analyze the nanostructured surface, various sophisticated techniques such as FESEM, EDAX, AFM, and electrochemical approaches are employed. Electrochemical approaches including DPV, EIS, and CV are applied to investigate the electroactivity of substrate, immobilization of Cap-223 on Au-GQD-NPs and subsequent hybridization with the miR-223. The electro-nanofabricated biosensor showcases a wide linear detection range (zepto M to nano M) with an impressively suitable LOD (0.024 atto M). The biosensor selectivity is demonstrated through discrimination studies between target and mismatched miRNA sequences (miR-9 and miR-21). This biosensor also exhibits significant potential for quantitative evaluation of miR-223 in real samples, enabling its applications in point-of-care (POC) detection of CRC. In addition, the Au-GQD-NS substrate is nano-fabricated in absent of surfactants and stabilizers, providing secure anchors for immobilization of cap-223. The well-established strategies for cap-223 immobilization and hybridization with miR, combined with superior electron exchange rate compared to uncovered FTO, upgrade the biosensor’s execution. Significantly, the biosensor demonstrates efficient miR-223 detection in human serum. Overall, this nano-fabricated biosensor shows great promise for miR-based early detection of CRC, and therefore, offering great sensitivity, selectivity, and favorable hydrophilicity for translational medicine investigation and clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) positions as the 3rd most predominant cancer universally and known as the 2nd moment driving cause of cancer-related mortality. Early detection of CRC, especially through biomarkers, holds immense significance due to its positive impact on treatment outcomes and overall survival rates. The majority of advanced stages of CRC detected and usually treated with surgery and systemic chemotherapy, while the metastasis occurred and these treatment approaches may not be helpful. Current treatment of CRC often leads to recurrent cancer that is resistant to chemotherapy, resulting in a small percentage of long-term survivors. [1]. While known genes have provided valuable insights, previously unknown noncoding RNAs, such as microRNAs (miRNAs), may offer further understanding of cancer biology. Researchers are exploring the use of miRNAs as a novel strategy for early cancer detection. These small RNA molecules play crucial roles in cellular, and metabolic activity regulations, including control of cell cycle regulations, and differentiation, proliferation, and cells survival. Uncontrolled miRs expression is associated with various diseases, including cancer [2]. The miRNA expression levels are used as a tool for diagnose of the cancer type, clinical stage, and other characteristics more efficiently than mRNAs. However, quantification and visualization of miRNAs expression pose challenges owing to their small size, high homology, and low abundance. Traditional methods like Northern blotting are time-consuming and require large RNA samples [2–5]. Although microarray techniques offer high throughput, they have limited sensitivity and long hybridization times. qPCR techniques are widely applied for analysis of miRNA due to their abilities (sensitivity and specificity). However, they are complex and need satisfied lab skills, with potential for false positives results during the process of amplification [6,7].Fig. 1. .

Detection of miRNA using smart Nano-based biosensors significantly advanced the early detection of colorectal cancer. Various NPs such as AuNPs [8–11], and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [12], have been explored for miRNA detection by researchers. These nanomaterials possess surface-area-to-volume aspect ratio, exceptional electroactivity, and notable physico-chemical stability, making them effective tools for enhancing classical detection methods. To develop novel and advanced nanobiosensors, investigators are focusing on smart and advanced nanomaterials with attractive surface chemistries hence, making them promising candidates for electrochemical biosensing [6]. Construction of nanostructures from graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles offers a synergistic effect, combining the unique properties of both nanomaterials to provide enhanced sensitivity, stability, biocompatibility, versatile surface properties, plasmonic effects, and signal amplification in electrochemical biosensing applications [13,14]. Nanparticles including GQDs and AuNPs are two types of nanomaterials that offer unique properties (enhanced stability, electroactivity, and other physio-chemical properties) when combined together, making them suitable for electrochemical biosensing applications [15]. The enhanced sensitivity of GQD and AuNPs possess excellent conductivity and large surface-to-volume ratio, which allows for efficient charge transfer and enhanced sensitivity in electrochemical biosensors [16]. Both nanomaterials have inherent stability, allowing them to maintain their properties under various environmental conditions. This stability is crucial for prolonged and reliable biosensing performance, ensuring the accuracy and consistency of the measurements [17]. The GQDs and AuNPs have tunable surface properties, makes versatile surface properties that they can be functionalized with numerous bio-macromolecules including DNA strands, Ab or Ag, and proteins or enzymes [16,18]. This functionalization enables specific and selective target diagnosis analytes binding in biosensing applications, making them useful for a wide range of diagnostic purposes. Signal amplification, especially in electrochemical biosensing is important for very low number of reagents or biomolecules detection [19]. This ability of QCDs and AuNPs can enhance limit of detection and also detect biomolecules in very low limit. There are several ways to synthesis the surface for electrochemical biosensing [20].

Developing the surfactant-free synthesis approaches for nanostructured surfaces is crucial for the advancement of electrochemical biosensors for cancer biomarker detection [21,22]. The omission of surfactants in the synthesis of nanostructured surfaces for electrochemical biosensors is preferred due to the enhanced electron transfer kinetics and improved sensitivity, reduced non-specific binding and fouling of the electrode surface, and increased accuracy and reliability of the biosensor [23]. The surfactants are typically used to regulate the size, shape, and surface properties control of NPs during synthesis. However, their presence can also affect the kinetics of electron transfer at the electrode-surface interface [24]. In electrochemical biosensors, the detection mechanism relies on the transfer of electrons between the biomarker and the electrode surface. The presence of surfactants can hinder the electron transfer process, leading to decreased sensitivity and lower overall performance. This can be attributed to the role of the surfactant layer on the electrode surface, which hinders direct contact between the biomarker and the electrode [25–27]. By avoiding the use of surfactants in the synthesis of nanostructured surfaces, the biomarker can directly interact with the electrode surface, allowing for more efficient electron transfer and higher sensitivity, this in turn leads to improved limit of detection (LOD), and enhanced selectivity [28]. Furthermore, the absence of surfactants can also reduce the likelihood of non-specific binding events and fouling of the electrode surface. Surfactants can lead to the adsorption of unwanted species, such as proteins or other biomolecules, which can interfere with the detection process and results in the decrease of the accuracy of the biosensor [29,30].

In this groundbreaking study, we introduce a cutting-edge label-free nanostructured electrochemical (nano) biosensor to have a highly sensitive for early detection of the CRC biomarker, micro RNA-223 (miR-223). Leveraging the notable characteristics of GQD and gold NPs (Au-GQD-NS), we have successfully overcome the long-standing challenge of weak stability faced by electrodeposition approaches in gold nanostructure fabricating, in addition providing surfactant-free approaches for fabrication of biosensor as high electroactive substrate. By incorporating graphene quantum dot on Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode, we not only achieved unprecedented stability of AuNPs, but also significantly improved the LOD to miR detection, even surpassing the performance of miRNA detection in standard solutions. Furthermore, our biosensor demonstrates exceptional performance in detecting miR-223 in real serum samples, emphasizing its ability as a strong tool for early diagnosis and improved management of the colorectal cancer.

The HAuCl4·3H2O, Glucose powder, Phosphate buffer saline (PBS), Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), dithiothreitol (DTT 0.1N), β-mercaptoethanol (βME), and absolute ethanol (99.5%), potassium ferricyanide [K3Fe (CN)6], potassium ferrocyanide [K4Fe (CN)6], potassium chloride (KCl) were acquired form Sigma-Aldrich, the FTO or Fluorine-doped tin oxide (2.2 mm thickness and 15 Ωsq- 1 surface resistance) were bought from Nanogostar Sepahan (I.R.Iran). The autoclaved ultrapure water (MilliQ water) provided from lab instrument (QWATER.CO, Qwater Premier Plus model). A Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum) was used to measure the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the GQDs thin films that were fabricated. The optical properties of the GQDs thin films were characterized using fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Cary 500 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer). Raman spectroscopy (Thermo scientific Nicolet) was utilized to verify the molecular structure. DLS was utilized to study the size of the GQD nanoparticles. The surface morphology of the GQDs thin film on FTO and Au-GQD-nanostructure was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu XRD-6000) and an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Veevo-Bruker 3100) in non-contact topography mode, as well as FESEM (Mira-3-XMU). Electrochemical measurements and depositions were carried out using an Origalys potentiostat/galvanostat system (BioLogic Inc., SP-300 EC-Lab®). Experiments involving biological samples were conducted in accordance with the guidelines approved by the University of Tarbiat Modares's Health Research Ethical Committee (Approval ID: IR.MODARES.REC. 1401.068). The serum was generously donated by our colleague, following ethical approval processes as outlined by the ethics committee at Tarbiat Modares University.

For the present work, the miR-223 oligonucleotides synthesized with its complementary capture-probe 223 (Cap-223, Bioneer, Republic of Korea). The buffer containing Tris–HCl at a concentration of ten mM and pH 7.5, along with EDTA (one mM), applied as capture probe solvent and the Cap-223 stored at −20 °C until it is ready to be used. Two separate solutions used for dissolving biomolecules entitled as S1, solution 1, and S2, solution 2, for Cap-223 and miR-223 respectively. The Cap-223 was put into solution number 1, which consisted of 1 M NaCl, 10 mM citric acid, and 10 mM Na2HPO4, and dissolved. The miRs were solubilized in solution number 2 comprising of NaCl (300 mM), and trisodium citrate (30 mM), adjusted for pH7–8 (for hybridization with Cap-223). The non-complementary miR-9 and 21 were also provided by same company. The end of the cap-223 oligonucleotide probe was modified with a thiol group (−SH). The lyophilized sample of the oligonucleotides was dissolved in Tris-Acetate (TA) buffer and stored at −20°C for use of the capture probe. To reduce the S-S bond, DTT (0.1N) was applied in a 1:1 ratio with the dissolved capture probe before use. Finally, our developed buffer was added to achieve the final molarity (will describe in next section, buffers). Each oligonucleotide had a master concentration of 100 pmole/μL. The sequences of the synthetic oligonucleotides were as follows:
The 5′ thiolated Cap-223 (5′TTTTTTTTTTTTGCACATAAACTGTTCGACTCAA-3′),
Hsa-miR-223 (5′-CGUGUAUUUGACAAGCUGAGUU-3′).
The miR-9 (5′-UCUUUGGUUAUCUAGCUGUAUGA-3′).
The miR-21 (5-́UAGCUUAUCAGACUGAUGUUGA-3́).

To synthesize the water-soluble GQDs, a glucose powder (0.5 M) was dissolved in deionized distilled water (4 g in 40 mL of DDW) with continuous stirring at 600 rpm. The solution was then transferred to an autoclave reactor and heated at 160-170° C for 6–8 h. Before heating in autoclave reactor, the solvent subjected to heating in a conventional microwave oven at a specific power level for 15 min (in glass bottle). Finally, the autoclave reactor kept at RT to cool down. To further concentrate the solvent, it was kept at 70° C to facilitate the evaporation of excess water [31].Electrochemical procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of GQD and Au-NPs on the FTO
ce:section.ce:para: To this end, we have originated a fast, simple, and reliable nanostructure platform for the detection of CRC biomarker (miR-223). The fabrication process of the gold nanostructure involved several steps. First, the FTO bare electrodes were cleaned with water and then treated with soap detergent, acetone, and ethanol in ultrasonication water bath at 70° C. After thorough washing (DDW), a thin film layer of graphene quantum dot nanosphere (GQD) was electrochemically deposited onto the FTO electrodes. The process of synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) involved electrodeposition on FTO. The activated FTO surface was then applied as substrate for electrochemically GQDs deposition to fabricate a thin film layer of GQDs structure. A chronoamperometry techniques using a GQDs solution at a high voltage (15 min at 4 V) was used for fabrication of GQDs layer on the FTO electrodes. This resulted in an identical GQDs nanostructure on the FTO electrodes, which was subsequently washed 2–3 times with distilled deionized water (DDW) to remove any unattached GQDs. Finally, a chronoamperometry techniques was employed for depositing gold nanoparticles on the GQDs layer. A solution of HAuCl4·3H2O (0.5 mM) was utilized to fabricate the gold NPs at a low voltage (fifteen minutes and 0.1 V), followed by washing with DDW [32–34]. The electrochemical techniques including CV, and EIS were applied to characterize the nanostructured electrodes. Finally, FESEM, XRD, and AFM imaging techniques were used to analyze the surface of the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cap-223 immobilization on the nanostructured platform
ce:section.ce:para: In the next step, the surface immobilization of cap-223 (SH-ssDNA) was carried out using our established protocol [35] as mentioned before. Initially, we used fresh DTT 0.1N (1:1 ratio) in order to reduce the S-S bond of thiolated Cap-223, which was then mixed with the DTT and dissolved in S1 solution. A serial dilution of Cap-223, (0.1 to 1.5 μM range), were examined to determine the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments. A droplet of the Cap-223 solution was dropped on the NS electrode platform and left for 2 h before being rinsed with DDW. The electrochemical measurements such as CV and EIS and DPV were applied step-by-step. To prevent nonspecific binding, improve nucleotide hybridization, and allow immobilized ssDNA capture probes to be properly oriented, a solution containing 0.2 mM βME in methanol was applied to block unreacted Nanoislands on the electrode surface. To evaluate the sensitivity of the nanostructured electrode, various concentrations of miR-223 (zM to μM) were applied followed by measuring the EIS and DPV and the records were compared with bare nanostructured electrodes. Each measurement was repeated at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MiR-223 assay in the real serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was employed to test the efficiency of the nanostructured biosensor in human serum. Consequently, various concentrations of miR-223 were introduced to human serum samples (fifty-fold diluted in PBS) and the miR-223 detected in serum samples. All measurements, including EIS and DPV, were conducted at least three times for accuracy.

To find out the chemical structure of GQDs, we used FTIR spectroscopy to analyze their bonding composition and functional groups. By comparing the FTIR spectra of GQDs with glucose, we can determine the changes in functional groups (Fig. 2 -d). During hydrothermal carbonization, some glucose linkages break, resulting in hydroxy functional group (–OH) which contribute to intermolecular H-H bonding and high solubility in aqueous solutions, making GQDs suitable for various applications in biosystems. The peaks observed in the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can provide information about their functional groups and structural properties. In the FTIR graph we have observed a peak at 3432 cm−1 that suggests the existence of hydroxyl (–OH) groups, which are commonly found in GO and GQDs due to the oxidation process during their synthesis. In addition, a peak present at 2923 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of aliphatic C-H bonds. This peak indicates the existence of carbon-based chemical groups, such as sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the GQDs. The peak at 1641 cm−1 indicates the attendance of stretching vibrations of carbonyl group (C=O), which these carbonyl groups might be associated with carboxylic acid or ketone functional groups. The FTIR spectrum peak at 1034 cm−1 relates to the stretching vibration of carbon–oxygen bonds (C-O), suggesting that the GQDs might contain surface functionalized with oxygen-containing groups. The peak at 700 cm−1 can be attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibrations of carbon atoms in the GQDs. This peak is often observed in graphene-based materials and serves as a characteristic feature of graphene and graphene derivatives. Overall, the observed peaks in the FTIR spectrum suggest the successful synthesis of GQDs with functional groups such as hydroxyl groups, aliphatic C-H bonds, carbonyl groups, and oxygen-containing groups. The presence of these functional groups indicates potential surface functionalization and provides insights into the chemical features and potential applications of the GQDs [36].

The optical properties of GQDs were investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum of GQDs displayed a prominent absorption peak around 250 nm, representing the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, and another peak around 270 nm, attributed to the n-π* transition. The spectrum also exhibited a longer shoulder extending into the visible region (Fig. 2-a). These GQDs demonstrated exceptional photo-stability even under harsh conditions. Typically, GQDs exhibit an absorbance peak in the UV range (200–300 nm) due to the π-π* transition of CvC bonds with sp2 hybridization, and occasionally a weaker tail at 270–400 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of CvO bonds, extending into visible wavelengths. The spectrum of UV–Vis were further confirmed the absorbance peaks at around 270 nm, corresponding to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of CvC and CvO bonds, respectively. The electronic transition of aromatic sp2 domains leading to the formation of delocalized π states on the basal planes, similar to graphene, is indicated by the π → π* transition. The fluorescence spectra of GQDs dispersed in water showed broad absorption ranging from 300 to 800 nm (Fig. 2-b). Additionally, the highest peaks did not change when the excitation wavelength was between 340 and 420 nm. This is because the sp2 clusters in GQDs have consistent size and surface states, which are responsible for their fluorescence characteristics. The GQD solution remained homogeneous for a duration of 3 months, with no observable changes in fluorescence, even when stored at room temperature [37,38].

The study utilized Raman spectroscopy [39], to analyze the level of structural defects in the carbon matrix. Our analysis concentrated on a range of imperfections such as grain boundaries, point defects, stress, strain, stacking faults, edges, doping, vacancies, and functional groups. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs displayed prominent characteristic bands representing defects in the graphene basal plane (Fig. 2-c), with a high intensity ratio indicating a partially defects in crystal structure due to the small sp2 cluster size [38]. Additionally, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was employed to determine the hydrodynamic sizes of the GQDs (Fig. 2-e), which had an average size distribution of approximately 89 nm. Multiple measurements were conducted to ensure accuracy, and the obtained particle size was compatible with measurements obtained using FESEM, indicating that the emission characteristics of the GQDs were influenced by the quantum size effect and the recombination of holes and electrons. It is worth noting that ultrasonic treatment was utilized to prevent particle aggregation before analysis [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of surface through FESEM-EDAX, AFM and XRD
ce:section.ce:para: The use of robust substrates with an excellent surface to area and greater electroactivity enhances the performance of electrochemical biosensors. Compared to bulk materials, nanostructured platforms offer superior properties. In current work, we nano-fabricated a strong substrate with a large surface area and strong electro-conductivity by utilizing a nanofabricated GQD-based nano-particle decorated with gold nanoflowers. The surface properties of the substrate, including crystallography and morphology of the Au-GQD- NS, were characterized by techniques such as AFM, GI-XRD, and FESEM [33].

XRD crystallographic characterization of the Au-GQD- NS was performed using a Shimadzu XRD-6000 instrument equipped with a CuKα source. The incidence angle was set to 1°, and 2θ values was set from 0° to 80° range. [41,42]. As depicted in Fig. 3 -a distinct (002) signal was observed at around 26°, which is related to an interlayer spacing of 0.334 nm, in the XRD pattern of the GQDs alone. In contrast, the XRD pattern of the Au-GQD-NS exhibited a weak broad (002) peak, indicating the presence of some thin and disorderedly stacked GQDs. Fig. 3-a illustrates the XRD patterns of FTO-GQD and Au-GQD thin films. The diffraction peaks of the FTO substrate were observed at 2θ = 26.7°, 33.8°, 38.0°, 51.7°, 55.0°, 61.7°, 66.1°, and 78.7°, corresponding to the planes of (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (310), (301), and (321) of SnO2, respectively, as reported in the literature (ICDD 01–070-4176). The XRD analysis confirmed the existence of GQDs, and the peaks corresponding to FTO were more pronounced, possibly due to the semiconducting nature of GQDs. The intensity of the peaks slightly increased after gold coating, indicating improved conductivity of the substrate due to the presence of gold atoms.

The surface of nano-fabricated patterns on the FTO electrode was examined using FESEM. Fig. 4 shows that the GQDs formed nanosphere-like structures with protrusions, likely due to the aggregation of GQDs, and exhibited nanoscale dimensions. FESEM images in Fig. 4 also revealed the presence of gold nanoparticles on the surface of the GQD nanospheres, providing a larger surface area for the Cap-223 landing on the surface. The images, along with the GIXRD patterns, firmly establish the successful fabrication of the Au-GQD nanostructure, showcasing an outstanding surface area. These results are in line with the findings of FESEM analysis. The increased surface area, in addition enhanced roughness at the nanoscale demonstrated in the FESEM images enhance the possibility of immobilizing more Cap-223 molecules on the surface, thereby increasing stability and improving biosensor sensitivity. The EDAX mapping clearly showed the presence and percentage of elements in the fabricated substrate [43,44]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) mapping using FESEM of graphene quantum dot layer coated FTO showed peaks including 0.5 keV (C Kα), 0.5 keV (O Kα), 1.8 keV (Si Kα), 3.5 keV (Sn Lα), and 3.8 keV (Sn Lβ). While the GQD and gold nanoparticles coated on the FTO showed same peaks, in addition different peak at 2.2 keV (AuMα), and 2.3 keV (AuMβ), and 9.5 keV (AuLα). Based on the information shown in Fig. 4, the peaks observed at 0.5 keV represent the characteristic X-ray emissions from carbon (C Kα) and oxygen (O Kα) present in the GQD layer. Additionally, peaks at higher energies of 1.8 keV (Si Kα), 3.5 keV (Sn Lα), and 3.8 keV (Sn Lβ) indicate the presence of silicon and tin, likely originating from the FTO substrate. Based on the EDAXpattern of Fig. 4-i and h, it can state that the GQD layer is also coated with gold nanoparticles. The peaks observed from the Au-GQD combination are similar to the GQD layer peaks mentioned above (0.5 keV for C Kα and O Kα, and 1.8 keV for Si Kα, 3.5 keV for Sn Lα, and 3.8 keV for Sn Lβ). These peaks indicate that the GQD layer is still present. However, there are additional peaks observed at 2.2 keV (AuMα), 2.3 keV (AuMβ), and 9.5 keV (AuLα). These peaks are specific to the characteristic X-ray emissions from gold (Au). Therefore, the additional peaks indicate the presence of gold nanoparticles on top of the GQD layer. The peak at 2.2 keV (AuMα) corresponds to the Mα transition in gold, while the peak at 2.3 keV (AuMβ) corresponds to the Mβ transition. The peak at 9.5 keV (AuLα) corresponds to the Lα transition in gold. In conclusion, the presence of these additional peaks indicates that gold nanoparticles are successfully coated on top of the GQD layer, allowing for the detection of their characteristic X-ray emissions.

The results achieved from the AFM characterization of the substrate are consistent with the changes in the substrate. The AFM height profile measurements clearly indicate variations among the different samples, with the bare FTO substrate displaying different heights compared to the pre-seeded and electrodeposited FTO samples containing GQDs and Au-GQDs. Additionally, the FESEM of the samples provides further evidence of the annealing process of the gold deposited on the FTO substrate, followed by the growth of gold nanospheres (AuNSs) through electrodeposition. These findings validate the successful production of nanostructures with an excellent surface area and roughness. Utilizing nanostructures with the mentioned criteria, more molecular cap-223 can be immobilized to the substrate, resulted in improving the sensitivity of the nanobiosensor. Fig. 5 presents AFM images of the GQDs- FTO and Au-GQD-FTO. The FTO-GQDs surfaces appear more uniform compared to the Au-GQDs nanostructures. The average roughness of substrates is also illustrated in Fig. 5. The substrate of FTO electrodes revealed a smooth surface, with surface roughness values ranged from 11.9 to 22.2 nm, while the GQDs introduced as thin film the roughness increased around 217.6 nm. Although the increased roughness may impact the thermal-conductivity of GQDs thin films negatively, it is crucial for enhancing the surface to volume ratio. The presence of gold on the surface results in an increase in surface roughness and connection ability [45–47].

The results from the DPV analysis in Fig. 6 ,b, were consistent with those from the CV (Fig. 6,a) analysis, show a reduction in the height of CV peak with each step. The EIS analysis in Fig. 6-b exhibited an increase in the peak height for EIS (−Z″, Ω) and a Z′ (Ω) shift. These changes in CV, EIS, and DPV approved the miR-223 detection hybridized on the nanostructure platform. The Nyquist Z curves demonstrate electron-transfer through impedance spectra [48], with an increase in repulsion force and electron-transfer resistance as an increasing in the number of phosphate skeletons of miR-223 hybridized on the nanostructured biosensor [49]. EIS serves as an “electrochemical electrode surface imaging method” by providing surface characterization, including surface charge, electrode–electrolyte interaction, and electrode kinetics [50]. The Rct value shows an inverse relationship with the electron transfer rate, while low frequency Warburg diffusion component (W) suggests ion diffusion [51]. The CV, DPV, and EIS assays were applied to screen the electrochemical changes of the experiment in each step. Fig. 6 shows the corresponding graphs. The CV, DPV and EIS parameters approved the successful nanofabrication of the Au-GQD-NSs, the Cap-223 immobilization, and the miR-223 hybridization withCap-223. To this end, the redox probe (CN)6]-4/[Fe(CN)6]-3 used for assays and were performed within the potential range of −0.3–––0.6 V. In Fig. 6a; the CV analysis shows the hybridization of miR-223 with the capture probe (green curve). In Fig. 6, c; the EIS assays show similar results to the CV data, with increased potential peaks for Au-GQD-NSs-FTO compared to cap-223 and miR-223. The Rct increases and the speed of electron transmission decreases after the immobilization of cap-223 and the hybridization of miR-223 [52]. The electron-transfer resistance (Ret) can be determined by analyzing the diameter of the semicircle section in EIS Nyquist plots, while the diffusion-limited process is reflected in the linear portion at lower frequencies. Results from DPV analysis, as shown in Fig. 6,b, were found to align with those from CV analysis at each step, with a noticeable decrease in peak height observed in each subsequent DPV graph. Parameters for optimal miR-223 detection in a biosensor were carefully selected. Factors like pH, temperature, and probe concentrations were varied to understand their impact on sensor response. Previous literature and initial studies guided the selection of these parameters. pH was adjusted to improve DNA probe-miR-223 binding for better sensitivity and selectivity. Temperature was controlled to maintain nanoplatform stability and aid in electron transfer. Probe and miR-223 concentrations were optimized for a wide detection range and low limits. This systematic approach greatly enhanced the biosensor performance for miR-223 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensitivity estimation of biosensor to miR-223
ce:section.ce:para: The electro-conductivity of the nanofabricated surface is of critical importance for electrochemical biosensors as it enhances the performance of various electrochemical techniques such as CV, EIS, and DPV. The electro-conductivity of the nanofabricated substrate before and after nanofabrication exhibited a significant increase, as shown in Fig. 7 . The enhanced electro-conductivity of the Au-GQD-NS platform indicates the successful nanofabrication process on the FTO electrodes. This in turn, enhanced biosensing efficacy of substrates by detecting tiny changes in resistance on the platform substrate. The CV technique was applied to record the changes in the potential of the working nanofabricated FTO electrode. The data achieved by electrochemical techniques demonstrated a significant change in the working electrode before and after nano-fabrication, as well as at each step of the process. In the present study, a 5′ thiol-end cap-223 (single strand DNA sequence) complementary to miR-223 immobilized on the nanostructures of the Au-GQD-NS, covalently. The Cap-223 was bonded to the Au-GQD-NS surface via disulfide bonding, enabling it to interact to its complementary sequence, miR-223, through hydrogen bonding. The EIS, and DPV measurements of both the bare Au-GQD-NS substrate and the functionalized electrode with Cap-223 demonstrated changes on the substrate, indicating the fortunate Cap-223 anchoring on the nanostructure (Fig. 7). In order to utilize additional free areas without obstructing the Cap-223 coating, a blocking agent, 2-mercaptoethanol, was employed on the uncoated surface. This was chosen over bovine serum albumin (BSA) because it does not have any interaction with DNA capture probe, and its tiny molecule size results in smaller changes in electrochemical signals (EIS, DPC, and CV), providing more error-free data. The EIS and DPV techniques were then used to monitor the changes that appeared on the substrate step-by-step from DNA capture probe immobilization to microRNA-223 final concentration (μM) detection. The data of EIS, and DPV measurements are presented in Fig. 7-b and a, showing an increase in the impedance (Z'' in ohms) of EIS peaks in comparison with the nano-furnished platforms (bare working electrode) and a fall down of peaks height occurred in DPV due to increased resistance. (Fig. 7).

The LOD of the nano-fabricated immunosensor estimated using the EIS data for different concentrations of miR-223, based on the equation:
LOD = 3.3
Sy / S
,
where Sy is the standard deviation of the response (blank EISs), and S is the slope of the semi-log calibration curve.

In this work, a broad range of microRNA-223 concentrations (zeptomolar to macromolar) were tested, resulting in a calculated ultra-low LOD of 0.024 attomolar for the nanostructured biosensor. Comparing our fabricated nanostructured platform to the previously developed biosensors by other groups, we observed excellent efficiency, making it a promising candidate for diagnostic tools.

The results of our study (Table 1 ) shows that the nanostructured biosensor used for electrochemical sensing of miR-223 has good reproducibility, repeatability, and low variability between repeats. A significant difference was found between samples (p<0.05), indicating the biosensor consistently provides reliable results. The CV% values (3.16% to 3.81%) demonstrate good repeatability, while RSD% values (3.07% to 4.05%) support the consistency of the biosensor. The low SD values (10.33 to 49.69) indicate low variability between repeats, ensuring accuracy and precision. Overall, these results suggest that the nanostructured biosensor is a reliable tool for electrochemical sensing of miR-223 in case of early detection. This is particularly important in the field of bio-sensing, where precise and accurate measurements are crucial for various diagnostic and research applications. The excellent reproducibility, repeatability, and low variability observed in the results further underscore the potential of this biosensor as a valuable tool for bio-sensing applications.

In order to assess the sensitivity and performance of the immunosensor, DPV and EIS measurements were carried out at various concentrations of miR-223. A broad ranging from 10 zM to 200 µM of miR-223 were prepared and subsequently incubated on the electrode surface for the hybridization of miR-223 and cap-223, starting from low concentrations and gradually increasing. It is evident from Fig. 7.a that an increase in miR-223 concentration results in a decrease in current. The DPV data obtained from the 10 zM blank electrode confirmed the biosensor's ability to detect miR-223 at such low concentration. This observation was further supported by the EIS assays, which exhibited an increase in the Rct value as the concentration of miR-223 increased. To establish a linear relationship between the Rct and the logarithm of miR-223 concentration, a plot was designed, and the resulting graph demonstrated a linear relationship within the range of 10 zM to 200 nM (Fig. 7-b).

After fabricating the nanostructured biosensor, the conductivity of biosensors were estimated. Then, to evaluate the performance of the Au-GQD-NS immunosensor, sensitivity studies were conducted using EIS, and DPV records at varying microRNA-223 concentrations. To evaluate the specificity of the immunosensor, mismatch miR-9 and miR-21 sequnces were used instead of miR-223 at a concentration of pM under the same experimental conditions (selectivity test). The electrochemical scanning of miR-223 is presented in Fig. 8 . The results indicate that there were no interactions observed between miR-21, miR-9, and cap-223, in contrast to the previous results obtained with miR-223. The findings indicate that our immunosensor exhibits exceptional specificity towards the designated miRNA. The addition of miR-21 and miR-9 did not result in significant changes in the DPV and EIS peaks, indicating the specificity of our biosensor for miR-223. We employed DPV and EIS procedure to analyze the selectivity performance of the nano-fabricated immunosensor. The EIS records were more validated by the DPV records conducted in a solution containing [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– and KCl. The DPV peaks provided data about the electrochemical phenomena occurring at the nanofabricated electrode and solution interface, consistent with the EIS curve. The biosensor demonstrated excellent selectivity when exposed to mismatch miR-21 and miR-9, further highlighting its ability to distinguish microRNA-223 from other microRNAs of the human samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of miR-223 in real sample (human serum)

ce:section.ce:para: Several investigations have been conducted to develop biosensors for early cancer detection using miRNA molecules. In this work, a label-free immunosensor was designed for the early detection of the CRC biomarker miR-223. The biosensor demonstrated excellent conductivity, high sensitivity, biocompatibility, ease of synthesis, and stability. The fabrication process involved the synthesis of GQDs using glucose as the precursor, which were then layered onto an FTO electrode and decorated with gold nanoparticles. The validation of the procedures was confirmed through EIS and DPV analysis. The electrochemical records observed in the miR-223 immunosensor were results of the response generated by the hybridization of miR-223 macromolecules with Cap-223. The simple nanofabricating protocol of the nanostructured immunosensor exhibited a highly sensitive limit of detection (LOD) in the aM range and demonstrated robust capability in human serum, highlighting its potency for CRC early detection and assessment. The hydrophilicity of the electrode interface played a vital role in achieving satisfactory antifouling properties, particularly when a hydration layer was formed on the electrode surface. The presence of Au-GQD-NS surface significantly enhanced the electrochemical signals by providing a larger active surface area and enhanced electroactivity. Additionally, the Au nanostructure surface facilitated the immobilization of the ssDNA capture probe, promoting miR-223 hybridization. The Au-GQD-NS-based biosensor exhibited ultra-high sensitivity at the aM level with remarkable repeatability, offering great potential for point-of-care (POC) diagnosis. This biosynthesis method is cost-effective, exhibits excellent stability, and ensures good repeatability and reproducibility, making it highly advantageous. Overall, the surfactant-free synthesis of nanostructured electrodes for electrochemical biosensing offers improved sensitivity, stability, biocompatibility, simplified fabrication processes, and enhanced mass transport. These advantages make it a promising approach for the development of advanced biosensing platforms with increased performance and reliability.
